title: Theory and Discovery of Electrides
authors: Chengcheng Xiao, Nicholas Bristowe, Arash A. Mostofi
abstract: Electrides are materials with electrons localized at interstitial regions of the crystal lattice and have been identified as promising candidates for a variety of applications, including catalysis, electron emission, and superconductivity. We present a theoretical framework for the origin of interstitial electrons in electrides. We demonstrate that this theory can explain electride-like behavior in prototypical electrides, and we use it to develop descriptors for the high-throughput discovery of new inorganic electride candidates from first principles. We also show that the same concepts can explain electride-like behavior in other classes of material, including high-pressure electrides and organic electrides and, more broadly, provide an alternative understanding of F-center defects and solvated electrons.
summary: This paper establishes a theoretical framework based on multicentered bonding to explain how electrons localize in the interstitial spaces of crystals. By introducing a new descriptor (Ze), the authors enable the high-throughput discovery of new electride materials and provide a unified description for organic electrides and F-center defects.
main_problem: The lack of a general theoretical framework to explain the origin of electron localization at interstitial sites in electrides and the absence of a predictive descriptor for discovering new electride materials.
main_result: The authors develop a 'Multicentered Bonding Theory' and a new figure-of-merit (Ze) for high-throughput discovery, successfully identifying new potential electrides like Hf2Au and demonstrating that the theory extends to organic electrides and F-center defects.
method: The study uses a combination of analytical LCAO models, Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations, and an automated high-throughput screening workflow applied to the Materials Project database, utilizing Electron Localization Function (ELF) and Bader analysis.
model_or_system: The system focuses on electrides (materials with electrons in interstitial regions), including prototypical examples like Ca2N and high-pressure Na, as well as organic electrides and F-center defects in MgO, modeled via multicentered bonding orbitals.
key_observables: Electron Localization Function (ELF) maxima, integrated basin charge (Q_ELF), critical cage size (bc), and the electride figure of merit (Ze).
important_parameters: The ratio of interstitial region size to orbital size (b/r0), the principal quantum number (n), and the critical cage size (bc).
main_assumptions: The analytical model assumes a non-interacting Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO) approximation and uses hydrogenic ns-orbitals to approximate the tails of atomic wavefunctions.
figures_summary: Figures include energy level diagrams for orbital localization, charge density and band structure projections for Ca2N and hP4-Na, a workflow for the Ze descriptor, and structural/ELF visualizations for Hf2Au and organic electrides.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of electride identification, develops an analytical multicentered bonding theory, validates it with DFT on known electrides, presents a high-throughput screening workflow and new candidates, and finally generalizes the framework to organic systems and point defects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic energy level diagram (upper right panel) and corresponding wavefunction plots (upper left and bottom panels) for a square molecule with an s-orbital on each atom. The form of the lowest energy bonding orbital (σg) depends on the ratio of the size of the interstitial region (measured by the distance b between the center and the corner atoms) and the size of the orbital (measured by the position r0 of the maximum of its radial distribution function). When this ratio is less than a critical value, σg is localized in the interstitial region.

FIG. 2. (a) Upper panel: Electronic charge density associated with the band that crosses the Fermi level in Ca2N. It is localized between two layers at the center of an octahedron formed by Ca atoms, marked by the red crosses. Lower panel: zoom-in of the Ca octahedron. (b) Bandstructure of Ca2N projected onto Ca 4s-orbitals. The size of each red circle indicates the magnitude of the projection. (c) Upper panel: Electronic charge density associated with the band located between -8 eV and -2eV in hp4-Na. The centre of the interstitial cage is located at Wyckoff site c. Lower panel: zoom-in of the interstitial cage formed by the nearest and second-nearest Na atoms of the interstitial site. (d)...

Fig. 2(d) shows the band structure projected onto the 4s-orbitals of Na. The fully occupied bonding orbital between −8 eV and −2 eV is almost entirely composed of Na 4s-orbitals. The charge density associated with this band, shown in Fig. 2(c), demonstrates it to be localized within the interstitial regions discussed above, confirming that the interstitial electronic state in hP4-Na may be understood in terms of the multicentered bonding theory presented above. It is worth noting that the fully occupied top valence band also corresponds to an interstitial localized elec- tronic state that is composed of Na atomic 3p-orbitals (see Sec. IV of the SM). This suggests that it is also important...

Fig. 2(c) shows the hP4 phase of sodium (hP4-Na). This is a double hexagonal closed-packed structure (space group P63/mmc) that is stable at a hydrostatic pressure of 320 GPa [16]. There are four Na atoms in the prim- itive cell: two on the edge (Wyckoff site a) and two in- side the primitive cell (Wyckoff site d). There are also two interstitial sites (Wyckoff site c), each with three first nearest neighbor Na atoms forming a triangle with b = 1.67 ˚A and six second nearest neighbors forming a triangular prism with b = 1.9 ˚A (Fig. 2(c), bottom left). FIG. 3. Schematic workflow for the electride figure of merit Ze. First, we determine the positions of the local maxima of the ELF by computing its gradient and Hessian. Second, we determine whether any of these local maxima might be asso- ciated with multicenter bonding by computing the distance to shells of nearest neighbor atoms of the same elemental species, and selecting those with more than two atoms in the same shell. Third, we determine the total electronic charge QELF associated with an ELF local maximum by Bader partitioning the ELF and integrating the electronic charge density of the system over the Bader-like basin Ωassociated with it.
